coredata.dc:title: A mitochondrion-targeting fluorescent probe for hypochlorite anion in living cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wenjing
Wang, Yuying
Wu, Na
Feng, Wei
Li, Zhanxian
Wei, Liuhe
Yu, Mingming
coredata.dc:description: As momentous reactive oxygen species (ROS), it is necessary to develop high-sensitivity and high-specificity fluorescent probes for tracking hypochlorite anion (ClO−) in environmental and biological systems. Herein, a kind of red luminescent carbon dots (NS-dots) was synthesized by one-step solvothermal method to detect ClO− in PBS buffer solution (VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1, pH = 7.4). The NS-dots has high sensitivity and low detection limit (13.3 μmol/L) for detecting ClO− with linear range from 6.7 × 10−5 mol/L to 26.7 × 10−5 mol/L. Using Rhodamine B (31% at 520 nm in water) as a reference, the NS-dots have a fluorescence quantum yield of 7.2%. Intracellular photostability, mitochondrial targeting properties and the fluorescence imaging towards intracellular ClO− were demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Hypochlorite
Living cells
Mitochondrial localization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing characteristics of ClO− based on the NS-dots

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of NS-dots. (b) Diameter size distribution measured by TEM. (c) XRD spectrum of NS-dots. (d) FT-IR spectrum of NS-dots.

(a) XPS spectrum of NS-dots. (b) UV-vis absorption spectrum of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1). (c) Fluorescence spectra of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1) at different excitation wavelengths. (d) Fluorescence intensity of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1) at I 620 (means the intensity at 620 nm) as irradiation (λex = 520 nm) time prolonged.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectral change of NS-dots solution (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1) upon addition of different concentrations of ClO− (0–0.4 mmol/L). (b) Linear relationship between absorbance at 551 nm of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1) with different concentrations of ClO−. (c) Fluorescence emission spectral change of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1) in the presence of different concentrations of ClO−, λex = 520 nm. (d) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 620 nm of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1) with different concentrations of ClO−. Photographs of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1) with different concentrations of ClO− under natural light. (e) and UV (365 nm) lamp (f) (from left to right, the concentrations of ClO− in the system were 0–0.33 mmol/L, and the concentration interval is 0.033 mmol/L).

(a) Effect of pH values on the fluorescence intensity at 620 nm of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, Vpbs:VEtOH = 100:1) in the absence and presence of ClO− (0.33 mmol/L), λex = 520 nm. (b) Fluorescence intensity histogram of NS-dots (0.005 mg/mL, Vpbs:VEtOH = 100:1) in the presence of different anions (0.33 mmol/L, red columns) and continued addition of ClO− (0.33 mmol/L, green columns), λex = 520 nm. I620 means the fluorescence intensity at 620 nm.

Photostability imaging of NS-dots in HeLa cells. Incubation of HeLa cells with 20 μg/mL NS-dots for 6 h, bright field (a) and fluorescence field (b-h) images after 543 nm CW laser irradiation for 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 60 and 90 min. λex = 543 nm, λem = 600–650 nm.

(a) Bright field image. Confocal fluorescence images of HeLa cells stained with 120 μg/mL NS-dots for 30 min (b) and commercial mitochondrial-Tracker (100 nmol/L) for 30 min (c). Merged image of (b) and (c).
(a–c) HeLa cells were incubated with 20 μg/mL NS-dots for 6 h. (d–f) HeLa cells were incubated with 20 μg/mL NS-dots for 6 h and then with 2 mmol/L ClO− for 30 min.
Fluorescence lifetime (τ1) with different concentrations of ClO− (CClO −).
The cell viability of HeLa cells with 0, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35 and 40 μg/mL NS-dots by incubating 24 and 48 h respectively.

head.ce:title: A mitochondrion-targeting fluorescent probe for hypochlorite anion in living cells

Institutes of Biomedical Sciences & Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Institutes of Biomedical Sciences & Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemistry for Energy Materials, Fudan University, 220 Handan Road, Shanghai, 200433, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Wenjing Liu and Yuying Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As momentous reactive oxygen species (ROS), it is necessary to develop high-sensitivity and high-specificity fluorescent probes for tracking hypochlorite anion (ClO−) in environmental and biological systems. Herein, a kind of red luminescent carbon dots (NS-dots) was synthesized by one-step solvothermal method to detect ClO− in PBS buffer solution (VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1, pH = 7.4). The NS-dots has high sensitivity and low detection limit (13.3 μmol/L) for detecting ClO− with linear range from 6.7 × 10−5 mol/L to 26.7 × 10−5 mol/L. Using Rhodamine B (31% at 520 nm in water) as a reference, the NS-dots have a fluorescence quantum yield of 7.2%. Intracellular photostability, mitochondrial targeting properties and the fluorescence imaging towards intracellular ClO− were demonstrated.

A high-sensitivity and high-specificity red luminescent carbon dots was synthesized by one-step solvothermal method to detect ClO− in PBS buffer solution.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) play a vital role in physiology [1,2]. Normal levels of ROS can effectively regulate the immune system and improve the immune function of organisms, while excessive accumulation of ROS can destroy the structure of proteins in living organisms and lead to various diseases [3]. ClO− is an important component of ROS and is extensively used as a disinfectant and bleach in daily life [4,5]. Endogenous ClO− achieves antibacterial effects by preventing the invasion of pathogens. However, the abnormal levels of ClO− in organism can lead to tissue damage and various diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis, neurasthenia, arteriosclerosis, and cancer [6–10]. Therefore, it is necessary to use convenient tools to detect ClO− in the environment and biological system specifically and sensitively.

At present, the reported methods for detecting ClO− mainly include colorimetry, fluorescence, electrochemical and chromatography [11–18]. However, the complicated preparation process, poor water solubility and unsatisfactory biocompatibility limit the application of these methods in biological systems. Compared with the organic molecular fluorescent sensors [19–21], carbon dots (CDs) are widely used due to their excellent photochemical properties, biocompatibility, high stability and low toxicity [22–29]. However, weak tissue penetration caused by short-wavelength emission and excitation dependence limits its application in biology [30–34]. Therefore, development of CDs with a long-wavelength emission and good water solubility remains an important issue.

In this manuscript, a one-step solvothermal method was used to design and synthesize N,S-doped red-emitting carbon dots (NS-dots) with 4-(diethylamino)salicylaldehyde and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid as precursors, for specific detection of ClO− in the buffer solution and cells. The NS-dots exhibit excellent water solubility, photostability and excitation wavelength independence. Sensing characteristics towards ClO− demonstrate that the as-prepared NS-dots exhibit high sensitivity, low detection limit, and a wide range of pH applications. Additionally, NS-dots have good biocompatibility and it can be used for cell imaging and mitochondrial localization.

Morphologies and microstructures of the NS-dots were characterized by TEM and XRD. As shown in Fig. 1a and b, the NS-dots had spherical shape, which was well dispersed without apparent aggregation. The size of NS-dots distributes between 6.3 and 8.0 nm with an average diameter of 7.2 nm, and there are no obvious lattice fringes were found in the NS-dots. As displayed in the XRD pattern of NS-dots (Fig. 1c), despite the presence of diffraction peaks, there was no corresponding XRD standard pattern corresponding to it, so the morphology of the NS-dots was amorphous.

Functional groups of NS-dots were characterized through FTIR spectrometry. As shown in Fig. 1d, the peaks at 3464 and 3359 cm−1 were attributed to stretching vibration of OH and N-H; The peaks at 2924 and 2852 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching vibration of C-C; One sharp and distinct peak at 1634 cm−1 were attributed to CO stretching vibration; The peaks at 1511 and 1461 cm−1 were resulted from the breathing vibration of the benzene skeleton; The peaks at 1325, 1213 and 1045 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of C-N-H, C-O-H and CS [25]. All FTIR peaks indicated a successful doping of C, N, O and S atoms and there are phenolic hydroxyl, aromatic-NH2, and aldehyde groups in the NS-dots.

More detailed composition and elemental contents of NS-dots have been investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) [26,35]. As shown in Fig. 2a, the full XPS spectrum exhibited four dominant peaks at 282, 529, 396 and 165 eV, which can be attributed to C1s (85.70%), O1s (9.95%), N1s (3.68%) and S2p (0.66%). The C1 speaks located at 287.99, 285.07 and 284.60 eV, which were assigned to CO, C-N/C-O/C-S and C=C/C-C, respectively (Fig. S1a in Supporting Information). The O1s spectrum shows main peaks at 531.87 and 530.74 eV ascribed to CO and OC (Fig. S1b in Supporting Information). The N1s spectrum decomposed into three peaks at 399.61, 399.07, 398.37 eV, indicating the presence of NH, NC, C-N-C (Fig. S1c in Supporting Information). The S2p spectrum could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 168.91, 168.48 and 167.35 eV, corresponding to SO, S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 (Fig. S1d in Supporting Information). The results of XPS high resolution spectra confirmed that N and S atoms were successfully doped into the NS-dots.

Optical properties of NS-dots were investigated by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. In the UV region, there were two peaks at 247 nm and 301 nm, which could be attributed to the π–π* transition of the CC. In the visible region, one distinct peak at 551 nm was assigned to n–π* transition of the surface states, indicating the existence of C–O, C–S and C=N/C=O bonds (Fig. 2b) [36]. Further, we explored the fluorescence emission spectra of NS-dots at different excitation wavelengths. Fig. 2c showed that with the excitation wavelengths changing from 350 nm to 570 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks of the NS-dots remained stable at 620 nm, unlike most NS-dots in prior works, which could be attributed to the relative uniform surface state and size distribution of NS-dots [37,38]. As indicated in Fig. 2d, the NS-dots had excellent photostability. Meanwhile using Rhodamine B (Φs = 31% in water excited at 520 nm) as reference, the fluorescence quantum yield (Φx) of NS-dots was calculated to be about 7.2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing characteristics of ClO− based on the NS-dots
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the sensing ability of NS-dots towards ClO−, we added a serious of different concentrations of ClO− to the NS-dots solution (0.005 mg/mL, VPBS:VEtOH = 100:1, pH 7.4) and immediately measured the spectra of the system. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the absorbance at 551 nm is decreased gradually upon increasing concentrations of ClO−, linearity range from 0 to 4.0 × 10−4 mol/L (R2 = 0.9907) was obtained (Fig. 3b). Meanwhile, two well-defined isosbestic points were observed at 497 and 585 nm, demonstrating the formation of new species which may be related to the interaction between surface functional groups of NS-dots and ClO− [39]. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of NS-dots at 620 nm displayed gradual decline with the concentrations of ClO− increasing (Fig. 3c), which exhibited a good linear relationship from 6.7 × 10−5 to 26.7 × 10−5 mol/L (R2 = 0.9958) (Fig. 3d). Detection limit of NS-dots for ClO− was calculated as 13.3 μmol/L (Signal-to-noise ratio of 3) [40,41], much lower than the maximum concentration of ClO− (58.4 μmol/L) at the distal end of water distribution system regulated by the USEPA [42]. Further, the photographs in Fig. 3e and f show that as the ClO− concentration increased, the color of the solution changed from red to colorless under natural light and portable UV (365 nm) lamp excitation. Time-dependent fluorescence response of the NS-dots towards different concentrations of ClO− was carried out (Fig. 4a). It is clear that the reaction between NS-dots and ClO− were finished within half minute. Moreover, we investigated the performance of NS-dots at different pH values (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence intensity of the NS-dots in the range of pH from 4 to 7 remained stable. After adding ClO−, a significant decrease in fluorescence at 620 nm could be found in the pH range from 3 to 8, indicating that the NS-dots had a wide application range of pH.

To evaluate the selectivity of the NS-dots towards ClO−, we measured the fluorescence responses to potential interfering substances and ClO−, respectively. As exhibited in Figs. S2a and S2b in Supporting Information, metal ions and anions have no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity of the NS-dots at 620 nm, except for ClO−, which causes fluorescence quenching. Then, we investigated the fluorescence color change of NS-dots under portable UV (365 nm) lamp in the presence of different anions. As shown in Fig. S2c in Supporting Information, only ClO− can quench the fluorescence. Additionally, we evaluated the anti-interference ability of NS-dots. The results showed that the quenching effect of ClO− on NS-dots was not affected in the presence of metal ions (Fig. S3 in Supporting Information) and anions (Fig. 4b). All these results demonstrate that NS-dots monitoring ClO− was specific and still have good sensing properties in the presence of interfering ions. Compared with the published literature for monitoring ClO− (Table S1) [3,43–45], the as-synthesized NS-dots had fast response time, wide pH application range and stable red emission, which may broaden the application potential of the NS-dots.

In general, the quenching caused by the transfer of energy/the production of the ground state complex is called dynamic quenching/static quenching. The quenching mechanism of monitoring ClO− has been performed by testing fluorescence lifetimes while adding 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 μL of ClO− (0.1 mol/L) to the NS-dots solution. As shown in Table 1 , with the presence or absence of ClO−, the NS-dots all shows single lifetime (τ1) and the fitting constants (x2) are satisfied. When 0–6 μL ClO− were added, the fluorescence lifetime of NS-dots did not change significantly, which was consistent with the static quenching mechanism. The addition of 8 μL of ClO− significantly quenched the fluorescence of the NS-dots and the fluorescence lifetime signal was attenuated, with a slight decrease in τ1 compared to the absence of ClO−. However, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NS-dots changed significantly in the presence and absence of ClO−, so we reasonably speculate that new ground state complex is formed between quencher ClO− and NS-dots, which means that the ability to support dynamic quenching could not be performed. All these results illustrated that fluorescence quenching of the NS-dots caused by ClO− was attributed to static quenching [25,46].

We evaluated cytotoxicity of different concentrations of NS-dots in HeLa cells for 24 h and 48 h according to the MTT assay. As shown in Table 2 , the results indicated that the NS-dots exhibited low biotoxicity at bioimaging concentration (20 μg/mL).

For photostability imaging of NS-dots in living cells, Fig. 5a shows a bright field diagram, Fig. 5b–h were fluorescence field pictures after 543 nm CW laser irradiation of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 60, and 90 min. These experiments indicated that the morphology of the HeLa cells under laser irradiation remained good and there were no significant changes, and the fluorescence (the color on the picture was pseudo-color, not the true fluorescence of NS-dots) did not significantly quenched during working hours, demonstrating the potential application availability of NS-dots in living cells.

Then, the organelle-localization ability of NS-dots was studied. Treating HeLa cells with 20 μg/mL NS-dots for 6 h, and then incubate it with 100 nmol/L commercial mitochondria probe for 30 min. According to the results in Fig. 6 , the NS-dots had the ability of targeting mitochondria. In general, positively charged moiety can target mitochondria [47,48]. Considering there are strong acid group (benzsulfonic acid group) and strong electron pushing group (diethylamino group) in precursors, there may be tertiary ammonium salt unit on the surface of the NS-dots, which may make the NS-dots have the ability to target mitochondria.

The applicability of NS-dots monitoring ClO− in living cells was performed by confocal fluorescence imaging experiments. As shown in Fig. 7 , significant fluorescence emission was observed after incubation of NS-dots (20 μg/mL) in HeLa cells for 6 h, and the fluorescence was obviously annihilated after incubation with 2 mmol/L ClO− for 30 min, indicating the successful detection of exogenous ClO− in HeLa cells. All of these results indicated that the NS-dots has low cytotoxicity, the ability to monitor intracellular exogenous ClO−, and which could be competent as a novel material for targeting mitochondria.

In summary, we synthesized N, S-doped carbon dots (NS-dots) for monitoring ClO− using one-pot hydrothermal method. Quantitative detection of ClO− in the range of 6.7 × 10−5 to 26.7 × 10−5 mol/L was achieved with a low detection limit of 13.3 μmol/L. Furthermore, the good biocompatibility of the NS-dots including its good intracellular photostability, low cytotoxicity, ability to target mitochondria and the successful detection of exogenous ClO− in HeLa cells makes it more reasonable to conclude that this material may have great potential application ability.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Wenjing Liu: Resources, Methodology, Software, Validation. Yuying Wang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Na Wu: Visualization. Wei Feng: Conceptualization. Zhanxian Li: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Liuhe Wei: Writing - review & editing. Mingming Yu: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported financially by the NSFC (21601158, U1504203, U1704161), and Zhengzhou University (32210431).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118564.
